title: Persist task-filter preferences

brief.desiredBehavior: You're building the data layer of a task-manager app. Remember a valid active task filter when a new task manager is created, while safely falling back for absent or invalid stored values.
acceptanceCriteria: Changing the filter writes the selected value to storage.
A new task manager uses a valid stored filter.
An absent or invalid stored value falls back to all tasks.
constraints: Work in the TypeScript task-manager library; this fixture has no browser screen.
Use browser storage defensively and preserve existing behavior.
brief.story: A person chooses a task filter, leaves, and returns expecting that preference to still make sense. Stored values are outside the app's control, so an unexpected value must not break the view.
brief.example: Before: every new task manager starts at all tasks. After: a valid saved filter returns, while an invalid value still shows all tasks.

planQuestions: Which preference should still be active when the task manager is created again?
Where does the task manager read and write that preference?
Which stored values must fall back safely?

hints.text: Think about when a preference is read versus when it is written.
hints.concept: A preference has a read moment and a write moment.

hints.text: What values should storage be allowed to restore?
hints.concept: Stored data needs a trusted set of values.
hints.lookAt: The boundary between stored text and the filter state.

hints.text: Inspect the filter state initialization and selection method.
hints.concept: Validation belongs where stored text becomes state.
hints.lookAt: The filter state initialization and selection method.
hints.testIdea: Create a new task manager with both valid and invalid stored values.

explainBackQuestion: Why should a stored preference be validated before it changes the UI?
